section.title: Spiritual Blessings in Christ
section.sectionRationale: This is the doorway into the whole letter, one long sentence in the Greek that pours out praise for what the triune God has done in Christ. Before Paul says a single word about how the church should live, he floods the reader with grace: the Father chose and adopted us, the Son redeemed us by His blood, and the Spirit sealed us as a pledge of what is still to come. Everything the rest of Ephesians asks of us flows from this opening flood of blessing.
section.thesis: The God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ has blessed us with every spiritual blessing in Christ, choosing and adopting us before the foundation of the world, redeeming us through the blood of the Son, and sealing us with the Holy Spirit as the pledge of our inheritance, all of it to the praise of His glorious grace.
section.frameNote: Four movements shaped by the Trinity: the greeting from Paul to the saints; the Father who chose and adopted us in love; the Son in whom we have redemption and the gathering up of all things; and the Spirit who seals us and guarantees our inheritance.

units.label: Greeting
units.note: grace and peace to the saints

verses.text: Paul, an apostle of Christ Jesus by the will of God, To the saints in Ephesus, the faithful in Christ Jesus:
Grace and peace to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

units.label: Chosen and adopted by the Father
units.note: before the foundation of the world

verses.text: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed us in Christ with every spiritual {{eulogia|blessing}} in the heavenly realms.
For He {{eklegomai|chose}} us in Him before the foundation of the world to be holy and blameless in His presence. In love
He {{proorizo|predestined}} us for {{huiothesia|adoption}} as His sons through Jesus Christ, according to the good pleasure of His will,
to the praise of His glorious grace, which He has freely given us in the Beloved One.

units.label: Redeemed in the Son
units.note: forgiveness and the gathering of all things

verses.text: In Him we have {{apolutrosis|redemption}} through His blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of His grace
that He lavished on us with all wisdom and understanding.
And He has made known to us the {{mysterion|mystery}} of His will according to His good pleasure, which He purposed in Christ
as a plan for the fullness of time, to bring all things in heaven and on earth {{anakephalaioo|together}} in Christ.
In Him we were also chosen as God's own, having been predestined according to the plan of Him who works out everything by the counsel of His will,
in order that we, who were the first to hope in Christ, would be for the praise of His glory.
And in Him, having heard and believed the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation, you were {{sphragizo|sealed}} with the promised Holy Spirit,
who is the {{arrabon|pledge}} of our inheritance until the redemption of those who are God's possession, to the praise of His glory.

eulogia.gloss: Blessing, a good word spoken and the good gift it conveys. The related verb runs three times through verse 3: the God who is blessed has blessed us with every blessing. These are spiritual blessings in the heavenly realms, not primarily earthly comforts but the whole treasure of salvation already ours in Christ, election, adoption, redemption, the sealing Spirit, and the inheritance to come.

eklegomai.gloss: To choose out, to select for oneself. God chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world, so that election is grounded in His own free love and purpose rather than in anything He foresaw in us. The goal of the choosing is stated at once: that we should be holy and blameless in His presence.

proorizo.gloss: To mark out beforehand, to determine in advance. Paul says God predestined us for adoption according to the good pleasure of His will. The word stresses God's prior, purposeful decision, and Paul always frames it in Christ and toward a goal, sonship and the praise of God's glory, never as cold fate.

huiothesia.gloss: Adoption as sons, the legal placing of an outsider into the full status and inheritance of a child. In the Roman world an adopted son gained the family name, rights, and estate. God predestined us for this: not merely forgiven servants but sons and heirs, brought into His family through Jesus Christ.

apolutrosis.gloss: Redemption, release secured by the payment of a ransom, the language of buying a slave or captive into freedom. In Christ this redemption comes through His blood and is defined at once as the forgiveness of our trespasses. The same word returns in verse 14 for the final redemption of God's possession, the completion of what the cross began.

mysterion.gloss: Mystery, a purpose once hidden in God and now openly revealed. It is not a riddle to be solved but a secret God has disclosed: His plan to sum up all things in Christ. Later in Ephesians the mystery includes Jew and Gentile made one in Christ, the unfolding of this single saving purpose.

anakephalaioo.gloss: To sum up, to bring together under one head. God's plan for the fullness of time is to gather all things in heaven and on earth into unity under Christ as head. The shattered cosmos, fractured by sin, is to be reunited and reordered in the Son, the goal toward which all history moves.

sphragizo.gloss: To seal, to stamp with a mark of ownership, authenticity, and protection. When they believed, God sealed the Ephesians with the promised Holy Spirit, marking them as His own and securing them until the day of redemption. The seal is not a thing but a Person, the Spirit Himself given to God's people.

arrabon.gloss: A pledge, deposit, or down payment that guarantees the full amount will follow. The Holy Spirit is God's first installment of the inheritance, a real foretaste that secures the whole. In later Greek the same word came to mean an engagement ring, a fitting picture of the Spirit as God's binding promise of the glory to come.

basic.context: Ephesians opens not with a problem to fix but with praise to pour out. In one long, breathless sentence Paul blesses God for every blessing He has given us in Christ. He traces salvation through the whole Trinity: the Father chose and adopted us before the world began, the Son redeemed us by His blood and forgives our sins, and the Spirit seals us as God's own and guarantees the inheritance still to come. All of it is to the praise of God's glorious grace.
basic.hermeneutics: This is worship before it is teaching. Paul is not laying out a cold system but overflowing with gratitude, and the doctrines of election, adoption, and redemption come to us wrapped in praise. Notice the refrain, to the praise of His glory, marking off the Father's, the Son's, and the Spirit's work. Read it first as a hymn of thanks for what God has done, and only then as a map of how He saves.
basic.originalLanguages: The passage turns on rich words. God has blessed us with every eulogia, spiritual blessing. He eklegomai, chose, us in Christ before the foundation of the world, and proorizo, predestined, us for huiothesia, adoption as sons. In the Son we have apolutrosis, redemption, through His blood. God made known the mysterion, the once hidden purpose, to anakephalaioo, to sum up, all things in Christ. And the Spirit sphragizo, seals, us, given as the arrabon, the pledge, of our inheritance.
basic.typology: Redemption through blood recalls the Passover and the whole sacrificial system, where a life was given so God's people could go free, now fulfilled in the blood of Christ. Adoption as sons takes up Israel's calling as God's son and opens it to all who are in Christ. The sealing Spirit answers the long promise of the Spirit poured out in the last days, given now as the first fruits of the age to come.
basic.christ: Everything here happens in Christ, in Him, through Him. He is the Beloved One in whom the Father freely gives us grace, the one through whose blood we are redeemed and forgiven, the head under whom all things in heaven and earth will be gathered. The Father chose us in Him before the world began, and it is by belonging to Christ that we receive every spiritual blessing.
basic.god: The Father is the fountain of all this grace, blessing, choosing, adopting, and working out everything by the counsel of His will. He is a God of purpose and of lavish generosity, who plans salvation before creation and pours it out at the fullness of time. And He does it all to the praise of His glorious grace, so that His goodness is displayed and enjoyed forever.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 8 speaks of those God foreknew and predestined, adopted as sons and given the Spirit as first fruits. 2 Corinthians 1 calls the Spirit God's seal and pledge in our hearts. Colossians 1 names the redemption and forgiveness we have in the beloved Son.
basic.oneStory: This passage lifts the curtain on God's plan from before creation to the fullness of time. What was fractured at the fall is being gathered up again in Christ, all things in heaven and on earth summed up under one head. The Ephesians, and every believer, are caught up into that plan, chosen, redeemed, and sealed, a down payment on the day when the whole cosmos finds its unity in the Son.

context.literary: Verses 3 to 14 are a single flowing sentence in the Greek, a berakah or blessing modeled on Jewish praise. It falls into three main movements marked by the refrain to the praise of His glory: the Father's electing love, verses 3 to 6; the Son's redeeming work, verses 7 to 12; and the Spirit's sealing, verses 13 to 14. The whole stands as the overture to the letter, sounding themes Paul will develop throughout.
context.historical: Ephesus was a major city of the Roman province of Asia, home to the temple of Artemis and a hub of magic, imperial cult, and spiritual powers. Into that world Paul announces a God who has planned salvation before creation and who is gathering all things under Christ, far above every rival power. Many manuscripts lack the words in Ephesus, suggesting the letter may have circulated among several churches in the region.
context.canonical: This opening belongs with the great statements of God's saving purpose, Romans 8:29-30 and 2 Timothy 1:9. Its sweep, from election before the foundation of the world to the summing up of all things in Christ, frames the whole biblical story and grounds the practical teaching of the letter in the settled purpose of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To open the letter with worship, grounding the church's identity and unity in the eternal, triune purpose of God, so that believers know themselves chosen, redeemed, and sealed in Christ, and live to the praise of His glory.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This section is doxology and declaration, describing what God has done, not issuing commands. Yet it is deeply formative: knowing we are chosen, adopted, and sealed shapes the humility, assurance, and unity that Paul will call for in the chapters that follow. The one implied response is praise.

debates.question: What is the nature of election and predestination here?

views.label: Unconditional election
views.case: God chose us in Christ before the foundation of the world according to the good pleasure of His will, grounding the choice in His free grace rather than in anything foreseen in us; the goal is that we be holy and to the praise of His grace.

views.label: Election in view of foreseen faith
views.case: Some read God's prior knowledge as including the faith He foresaw, so that predestination honors the response He knew would come, while still insisting salvation is entirely of grace.

views.label: Corporate election in Christ
views.case: Others stress that the chosen ones are chosen in Him, so election is first of Christ and the people found in Him, and individuals share in it by being united to the Beloved One.

debates.bearing: Whatever the emphasis, Paul frames it all in Christ and to the praise of His glory: election is never cold fate but the free, loving purpose of the Father that leads to holiness, sonship, and worship.

debates.question: What does it mean to sum up all things in Christ?

views.label: Cosmic reunification under Christ
views.case: God's plan is to gather the fractured cosmos, things in heaven and on earth, into unity and right order under Christ as head, the reversal of the disintegration caused by sin.

views.label: The scope of Christ's lordship, not universal salvation
views.case: The summing up declares Christ's headship over all things, but the letter still calls individuals to faith and shows some remain sons of disobedience, so it is not a promise that every person is saved.

debates.bearing: The phrase magnifies the breadth of God's purpose in Christ, giving believers a cosmic hope, while the rest of Ephesians keeps the call to personal faith and holy living firmly in view.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's opening hymn is packed with weighty terms. The God who is blessed has blessed us with every eulogia, spiritual blessing, in the heavenly realms. He eklegomai, chose, us in Christ before the foundation of the world, an act of free love, and proorizo, marked us out beforehand, for huiothesia, adoption as sons, the full status and inheritance of children. In the Son we have apolutrosis, redemption by ransom, defined at once as the forgiveness of trespasses. God has made known the mysterion, the purpose once hidden and now revealed, His plan to anakephalaioo, to sum up under one head, all things in heaven and on earth in Christ. And when they believed, God sphragizo, sealed, the Ephesians with the promised Spirit, given as the arrabon, the down payment and guarantee of the inheritance to come.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfills the Passover and the sacrificial system, where a life was given so God's people could go free, now accomplished by the blood of Christ that secures forgiveness.
Takes up Israel's calling as God's son, Exodus 4:22 and Hosea 11:1, and opens the family of God to all who are in Christ, made sons and heirs.
Answers the promise of the Spirit poured out in the last days, Joel 2 and Ezekiel 36, given now as the first installment of the inheritance to come.

study.christ: Christ is the sphere and the center of every blessing named here. We are chosen in Him, adopted through Him, redeemed by His blood, forgiven in Him, and sealed as those who belong to Him. He is the Beloved One in whom the Father freely gives us grace, and He is the head under whom God purposes to gather all things in heaven and on earth. To be a Christian, Paul shows, is to be in Christ, and in Him the whole treasury of salvation is already ours.
study.god: The Father stands at the source of the whole passage: He blesses, chooses, predestines, adopts, lavishes grace, makes known His will, and works out everything by the counsel of His will. He is sovereign and purposeful, planning salvation before creation, and He is astonishingly generous, giving lavishly in the Beloved One. His ultimate aim is His own glory, that His grace would be praised, and in that aim our deepest joy and security are secured.

crossReferences.note: Those God foreknew He predestined, called, justified, and glorified, the same golden chain of God's saving purpose.
God seals us and gives the Spirit in our hearts as a pledge, parallel to the sealing and pledge of verses 13 to 14.
In the beloved Son we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins, echoing the redemption of verse 7.
Salvation by grace through faith, not of works, unfolds the grace praised throughout this opening.

study.oneStory: This passage opens a window onto the whole plan of God, from before the foundation of the world to the fullness of time. The story that fractured at the fall is here being gathered back together, all things in heaven and on earth summed up under Christ. The Father chose a people, the Son redeemed them, the Spirit sealed them, and the end of it all is the praise of His glory. Believers today are swept into that same ancient and unfolding purpose, a down payment now on the day the whole cosmos is made one in the Son.

quiz.q: Where and in whom are the blessings of this passage found?

options.text: In Christ, every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms.
options.note: The phrase in Christ, or in Him, runs through the whole passage as the sphere of blessing.

options.text: In our own effort to earn God's favor.
options.note: The blessings are freely given in the Beloved One, not earned.

options.text: Mainly in earthly wealth and comfort.
options.note: These are spiritual blessings in the heavenly realms, the treasures of salvation.

options.text: In the spiritual powers Ephesus feared.
options.note: Christ is above every power; the blessings are in Him alone.

quiz.q: When did God choose His people, according to verse 4?

options.text: Before the foundation of the world.
options.note: Election is grounded in God's eternal purpose, before creation.

options.text: After He saw that they would believe on their own.
options.note: A view some hold, but Paul roots the choice in the good pleasure of His will.

options.text: At the moment they were baptized.
options.note: The choosing is before the world's foundation, not at a later ritual.

options.text: God did not choose; it was all human decision.
options.note: The text plainly says He chose us in Him.

quiz.q: What does adoption as sons mean here?

options.text: God places us into His family with the full status and inheritance of children.
options.note: Adoption, huiothesia, grants the name, rights, and inheritance of a son.

options.text: We become servants who hope one day to earn a place.
options.note: Adoption gives sonship now, not merely servant status.

options.text: It is only a figure of speech with no real standing.
options.note: It is a real legal placing into God's family through Christ.

options.text: It means God adopts everyone regardless of Christ.
options.note: The adoption is through Jesus Christ, according to God's will.

quiz.q: How do we have redemption in the Son?

options.text: Through His blood, defined as the forgiveness of our trespasses.
options.note: Verse 7 grounds redemption in Christ's blood and equates it with forgiveness.

options.text: By God simply ignoring our sin at no cost.
options.note: Redemption is by ransom, the blood of Christ, not by overlooking sin.

options.text: By our own good works and religious effort.
options.note: Redemption is in Him, according to the riches of His grace.

options.text: By joining the right spiritual rituals.
options.note: It is through Christ's blood alone, freely given in grace.

quiz.q: What is God's plan for the fullness of time?

options.text: To sum up all things in heaven and on earth under Christ as head.
options.note: Verse 10 reveals the mystery of God's will to gather all things in Christ.

options.text: To rescue souls while abandoning the created world.
options.note: The plan embraces all things in heaven and on earth, not souls only.

options.text: To leave history without any purpose or goal.
options.note: History moves toward the summing up of all things in Christ.

options.text: To exalt the powers of the age above Christ.
options.note: All things are gathered under Christ, not under rival powers.

quiz.q: What role does the Holy Spirit play in verses 13 and 14?

options.text: He seals believers as God's own and is the pledge guaranteeing their inheritance.
options.note: The Spirit is the seal of ownership and the arrabon, the down payment on glory.

options.text: He is a reward given only after a life of good works.
options.note: The Spirit is given when they heard and believed, not earned later.

options.text: He is merely a symbol with no personal presence.
options.note: The seal is a Person, the promised Holy Spirit Himself.

options.text: He replaces the need for any future inheritance.
options.note: He is the pledge of an inheritance still to be fully received.

questions: Paul begins the letter not with commands but with praise for grace. How might starting your own prayers with thanks for what God has already done change the way you pray?
God chose you in Christ before the foundation of the world. How does knowing your salvation rests on His eternal purpose, not your performance, steady you when you feel like a failure?
Adoption means you are a son or daughter with a full inheritance, not a servant hoping to earn a place. Where do you still relate to God as a nervous servant rather than a beloved child?
Redemption came through the blood of Christ, at real cost. How does the price of your forgiveness deepen your sense of how loved you are?
The Spirit is God's down payment, a guarantee of the glory to come. When the future feels uncertain, how does the Spirit's presence now assure you of what is still ahead?

translationNotes.note: Several early manuscripts omit the words in Ephesus, which has led many to think the letter was a circular sent to churches around Ephesus; the BSB retains in Ephesus, as followed here.
In love at the end of verse 4 may modify what precedes, holy and blameless in love, or what follows, in love He predestined; either way it grounds God's choosing in love.
To bring together, anakephalaioo, means to sum up or gather under one head; the BSB renders it together to capture the uniting of all things in Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 1:1-14 reproduced as supplied. No dashes were present to convert. Inline tokens mark the nine supplied lexicon keys, each used once.
